message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

Independent VERIFIER #1 of 2. A pass-2 assessor reported:
TITLE: FeatureMatrix accepts non-numeric rows despite its numeric type contract
DIMENSION: public API & adapter-Protocol stability
SEVERITY: MEDIUM
FILE: src/assay_engine/contracts/features.py:17-30 
DESCRIPTION: FeatureMatrix declares rows: tuple[tuple[float, ...], ...] and the module docstring states output is 'a plain numeric FeatureMatrix ... so the engine consumes it without knowing what the features mean.' __post_init__ validates row COUNT and WIDTH but never that the cells are numeric. PoC verified: FeatureMatrix(unit_ids=('u1',), feature_names=('f',), rows=(('not-a-number',),)) is accepted. A FeatureBuilder adapter that emits strings/None therefore passes construction and run_study's ingest-time checks (which only validate unit_ids dedup/ghost ids, pipeline.py:330-340), letting non-numeric 'features' flow into any downstream numeric consumer and fail far from the source — a silent break on a minor adapter change, with the only contract signal being a type annotation the runtime ignores.
SUGGESTED FIX: Validate cell types in FeatureMatrix.__post_init__: reject any cell that is not a real, finite number (e.g. `if not isinstance(x, (int, float)) or isinstance(x, bool) or not math.isfinite(x): raise ValueError(...)`), mirroring the finite-float discipline already enforced in confirm._validate_finite and the canonical hashing path.
Independently determine if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the code; PoC where applicable. is_real=false if false positive / already handled / documented-justified scope. Recalibrate severity. Give evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	"""Feature-builder interface — optional dataset-specific features.
2	
3	The baseline toolkit provides generic builders (embeddings, similarity, etc.). When a
4	dataset has features only meaningful in its own domain, an adapter supplies them through a
5	:class:`FeatureBuilder`. Output is a plain numeric :class:`FeatureMatrix` keyed by unit id,
6	so the engine consumes it without knowing what the features mean.
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	from dataclasses import dataclass
12	from typing import Protocol, Sequence
13	
14	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
15	
16	
17	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
18	class FeatureMatrix:
19	    """Numeric features for a set of units. ``rows[i]`` corresponds to ``unit_ids[i]``."""
20	
21	    unit_ids: tuple[str, ...]
22	    feature_names: tuple[str, ...]
23	    rows: tuple[tuple[float, ...], ...]
24	
25	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
26	        if len(self.rows) != len(self.unit_ids):
27	            raise ValueError("FeatureMatrix row count must match unit_ids length")
28	        width = len(self.feature_names)
29	        if any(len(r) != width for r in self.rows):
30	            raise ValueError("FeatureMatrix rows must match feature_names width")
31	
32	
33	class FeatureBuilder(Protocol):
34	    """Build dataset-specific numeric features from a canonical corpus."""
35	
36	    def build(self, corpus: Corpus) -> FeatureMatrix:
37	        """Compute features for (a subset of) the corpus units. Must be deterministic."""
38	        ...
39	
40	    @property
41	    def provides(self) -> Sequence[str]:
42	        """Names of the features this builder produces (for provenance)."""
43	        ...
44

content.text: Now let me understand the key question: where does `feature_matrices` actually get *consumed* as numbers? It's recorded into the StudyResult at line 575. Let me check if anything in the engine actually does numeric computation on FeatureMatrix.rows, or if it's purely provenance/output.

content.content:
1	"""Dependency-light, deterministic numeric primitives — the baseline's raw material.
2	
3	These are the small, exact, NumPy-free building blocks the methodology calls "raw material"
4	for a baseline: similarity/distance and descriptive statistics over a
5	:class:`~assay_engine.contracts.features.FeatureMatrix`. They are deterministic (no RNG, no
6	iteration-order dependence) and pure, so a study can assemble a simple baseline — and the
7	engine can unit-test these invariants — with no heavy dependency.
8	
9	Studies needing performance or richer ML (embedding models, clustering, graph/topology) bring
10	their own builders implementing :class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineBuilder`; the
11	engine deliberately does not prescribe those choices (ADR-0002). Heavier engine helpers may be
12	added later under the optional ``baseline`` extra, but the contract here stays dependency-free.
13	"""
14	
15	from __future__ import annotations
16	
17	import math
18	from typing import Any, Sequence
19	
20	Vector = Sequence[float]
21	
22	
23	def _check_same_length(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> None:
24	    if len(a) != len(b):
25	        raise ValueError(f"vectors must have equal length; got {len(a)} and {len(b)}")
26	
27	
28	def _check_finite(*vectors: Vector) -> None:
29	    """Reject non-finite components. Without this a NaN/inf could flow into cosine and be
30	    silently clamped to a false 1.0 (audit #B6); consistent with descriptive_stats."""
31	    for vec in vectors:
32	        for x in vec:
33	            if not math.isfinite(x):
34	                raise ValueError(f"vector components must be finite; got {x}")
35	
36	
37	def dot(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> float:
38	    _check_same_length(a, b)
39	    return math.fsum(x * y for x, y in zip(a, b))
40	
41	
42	def l2_norm(a: Vector) -> float:
43	    return math.sqrt(math.fsum(x * x for x in a))
44	
45	
46	def _clamp_unit(x: float) -> float:
47	    """Clamp to [-1, 1] to absorb floating-point overshoot (e.g. self-similarity 1.0000…002)."""
48	    return max(-1.0, min(1.0, x))
49	
50	
51	def cosine_similarity(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> float:
52	    """Cosine similarity in [-1, 1]. A zero vector has no direction, so its similarity to
53	    anything is defined as 0.0 (rather than NaN). The result is clamped to [-1, 1] so float
54	    rounding cannot push it outside the documented range (audit #B3)."""
55	    _check_same_length(a, b)
56	    _check_finite(a, b)
57	    na, nb = l2_norm(a), l2_norm(b)
58	    if na == 0.0 or nb == 0.0:
59	        return 0.0
60	    return _clamp_unit(dot(a, b) / (na * nb))

content.text:
Key finding: within the engine, `FeatureMatrix` is never numerically consumed. It's appended to `feature_matrices`, recorded as provenance, and returned in `StudyResult` at line 575. The engine's own numeric primitives (`primitives.py`) operate on `Vector` inputs and *do* enforce finiteness independently (`_check_finite`, line 28-34). So within the engine, a non-numeric FeatureMatrix never silently corrupts a computation — primitives reject it at their own boundary.

Let me confirm the StudyResult serialization path and run the PoC to confirm acceptance and check whether serialization/canonical-hashing would catch it.

180	    """A typed metric-logging callback for :func:`run_study`'s best-effort tracker."""

183	        t.log_metric(rid, key, n)
184

content.text: PoC confirms acceptance. Now the critical question for severity: does this cause a *silent* failure far from source, or do downstream consumers reject it? Let me check the comparison: the canonical hashing path and schema.py (which the docstring references as the model the finding cites). Let me check how Corpus units validate their content, and whether there's a precedent that FeatureMatrix is meant to validate cells.

content.content:
60	    attributes: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
61	
62	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
63	        object.__setattr__(self, "attributes", freeze_mapping(self.attributes))
64	
65	
66	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
67	class Corpus:
68	    """The full canonical dataset handed to the baseline builder."""
69	
70	    units: tuple[Unit, ...]
71	    relations: tuple[Relation, ...] = ()
72	    metadata: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
73	
74	    def unit_ids(self) -> frozenset[str]:
75	        return frozenset(u.unit_id for u in self.units)
76	
77	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
78	        ids = [u.unit_id for u in self.units]
79	        if len(ids) != len(set(ids)):
80	            raise ValueError("Corpus contains duplicate unit_id values")
81	        # A closed canonical corpus must not contain relations to non-existent units
82	        # (audit pass 1, issue #3) — mirrors the duplicate-id and FeatureMatrix checks.
83	        known = set(ids)
84	        dangling = sorted(
85	            {e for r in self.relations for e in (r.source_id, r.target_id) if e not in known}
86	        )
87	        if dangling:
88	            raise ValueError(f"Corpus has relations referencing unknown unit_id(s): {dangling[:5]}")
89	        object.__setattr__(self, "metadata", freeze_mapping(self.metadata))
90

content.text:
This is informative. The contracts validate structural integrity in `__post_init__` (Corpus dedup/dangling, FeatureMatrix count/width). The codebase has a clear, established discipline of validating data invariants at construction in `__post_init__`, and the finite-float discipline is enforced everywhere numbers are *consumed* (`primitives.py` `_check_finite`, `confirm._validate_finite`).

Let me assess the actual harm. The key mitigating fact: the engine never numerically consumes FeatureMatrix.rows itself — it's provenance/output recorded into the trail and StudyResult. Let me verify whether the provenance recording at line 341-347 touches the row values (which would be where a serialization break could occur).

The provenance record at lines 341-347 only records `feature_names`, `n_units`, `provides` — not the row values. So even the trail doesn't touch the cells. The non-numeric values sit inertly in the returned `StudyResult.feature_matrices`.

Now let me check: is there a test that establishes the intended contract, and does primitives reject non-numeric in a clear way (so it's NOT silent if consumed)?

content.text: I now have a complete picture. Let me look at the e2e integration test that goes corpus → FeatureMatrix → baseline primitives, to confirm whether features actually feed primitives in real usage.

content.content:
1	"""End-to-end methodology composition (pure — always runs).
2	
3	Composes the real engine pieces into one run: corpus → FeatureMatrix → baseline primitives →
4	reproducible BaselineArtifact (determinism harness) → a locked, pre-registered hypothesis →
5	confirmatory test against a null distribution → a three-valued verdict. This is the methodology
6	the engine exists to support, exercised composed rather than as isolated units.
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	import datetime as _dt
12	
13	from assay_engine.baseline.determinism import build_baseline_artifact, corpus_fingerprint
14	from assay_engine.baseline.primitives import cosine_similarity_matrix, descriptive_stats
15	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus, Unit
16	from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import confirm_whole_corpus
17	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisKind, HypothesisOrigin
18	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
19	    LocalHmacAuthority,
20	    lock_hypothesis,
21	    verify_preregistration,
22	)
23	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import VerdictLabel
24	
25	
26	def _corpus() -> Corpus:
27	    return Corpus(units=tuple(Unit(f"u{i}", f"control text {i}") for i in range(6)))
28	
29	
30	def test_baseline_to_verdict_pipeline_supported():
31	    corpus = _corpus()
32	    # baseline: a tiny embedding-like feature matrix → similarity structure → summary stat
33	    rows = [[1.0, 0.0], [0.9, 0.1], [0.0, 1.0], [0.1, 0.9], [0.7, 0.7], [0.5, 0.5]]
34	    sim = cosine_similarity_matrix(rows)
35	    offdiag = [sim[i][j] for i in range(len(rows)) for j in range(len(rows)) if i != j]
36	    observed = descriptive_stats(offdiag)["mean"]
37	
38	    artifact = build_baseline_artifact(
39	        corpus, {"similarity_mean": observed}, component_versions={"builder": "e2e@1"}
40	    )

content.text:
I now have everything needed to make a calibrated judgment.

## Assessment

**The finding is factually accurate** — the PoC confirms `FeatureMatrix.__post_init__` accepts non-numeric cells (strings, None, NaN, bool), validating only row count and width. The type annotation `tuple[tuple[float, ...], ...]` is a runtime no-op.

**Key analysis of actual production risk:**

1. **The "silent failure far from source" claim is overstated.** Within the engine, `FeatureMatrix.rows` is *never numerically consumed*. In `pipeline.py`, the feature matrix from an adapter builder is: validated for id dedup/ghost ids (330-339), recorded as provenance (only `feature_names`/`n_units`/`provides` — not cell values, 341-347), and stashed into `StudyResult.feature_matrices` (575) as returned output/provenance. A non-numeric cell sits inertly and never corrupts a computation.

2. **Where features *are* consumed numerically — the baseline primitives — failures are LOUD, not silent.** PoC shows `cosine_similarity(['not-a-number'], ...)` raises `TypeError: must be real number, not str`, None raises `TypeError`, and NaN raises `ValueError: vector components must be finite`. The defense-in-depth (`_check_finite` at primitives.py:28-34, audit #B6) already catches exactly the corruption scenario the finding worries about, at the consumption boundary.

3. **However, the finding correctly identifies a real contract gap consistent with the codebase's own established discipline.** The codebase deliberately validates data invariants in `__post_init__` (Corpus dedup + dangling relations at schema.py:77-89, explicitly "mirrors the … FeatureMatrix checks"; FeatureMatrix count/width). A FeatureBuilder is an *adapter Protocol seam* (the engine's untrusted boundary), and the module docstring promises "a plain numeric FeatureMatrix." The contract makes a numeric promise the constructor doesn't enforce, so a buggy adapter's bad output can escape into a returned/persisted `StudyResult` and reach a non-engine downstream numeric consumer that lacks the primitives' guard.

**Severity recalibration: MEDIUM → LOW.** This is a genuine, not-yet-handled defect (pass-1 #14/#17 added shape checks but not cell-type checks), and the fix is well-justified and cheap, aligning FeatureMatrix with the codebase's existing finite-float discipline at adapter seams. But the assessor's MEDIUM rests on a "silent break flowing into any downstream numeric consumer" narrative that is mitigated for all *in-engine* consumers by existing loud guards. The realistic blast radius is a contract-vs-runtime mismatch at an adapter boundary affecting only external consumers of the returned matrix — a hardening gap, not a correctness-corrupting hole.

input.reasoning:
TRUE but over-severity. The core factual claim is verified: FeatureMatrix.__post_init__ (src/assay_engine/contracts/features.py:25-30) validates only row COUNT and WIDTH, never cell numericity, despite the annotation tuple[tuple[float,...],...] and the docstring's "plain numeric FeatureMatrix" promise. PoC confirms strings, None, NaN, and bool are all accepted at construction, and pipeline.py:330-339 only checks unit_id dedup/ghost ids, so an adapter's non-numeric output passes ingest. This is a genuine, not-yet-handled contract gap at an adapter Protocol seam (pass-1 #14/#17 added shape checks but stopped short of cell-type checks), and it is inconsistent with the codebase's own established discipline of enforcing invariants in __post_init__ (Corpus dedup/dangling at schema.py:77-89, which explicitly says it "mirrors … the FeatureMatrix checks") and of finite-float enforcement everywhere numbers are consumed (primitives._check_finite, confirm._validate_finite). The suggested fix is well-justified and cheap.

However, the assessor's MEDIUM rests on a "silent break flowing into any downstream numeric consumer, failing far from source" narrative that I could not substantiate for in-engine paths. FeatureMatrix.rows is never numerically consumed inside the engine: in pipeline.py the matrix is only validated for ids, recorded as provenance (feature_names/n_units only — not cell values, lines 341-347), and stashed into StudyResult.feature_matrices (line 575) as returned output. The actual numeric consumers — baseline primitives — already fail LOUD, not silent: PoC shows cosine_similarity(['not-a-number'],...) raises TypeError, None raises TypeError, and NaN raises ValueError 'vector components must be finite' (primitives._check_finite, audit #B6). So the corruption scenario the finding fears is already caught with defense-in-depth at the consumption boundary. The realistic blast radius is narrowed to a contract-vs-runtime mismatch affecting only EXTERNAL (non-engine) consumers of the returned/persisted matrix that lack their own guard — a hardening/contract-honesty gap, not a correctness-corrupting hole. Hence real but LOW.
input.evidence:
PoC (acceptance): `FeatureMatrix(unit_ids=('u1',), feature_names=('f',), rows=(('not-a-number',),))` is accepted; same for None, float('nan'), and True. Confirms src/assay_engine/contracts/features.py:25-30 validates only count (len(rows)!=len(unit_ids)) and width (len(r)!=width), no cell-type check.

PoC (loud at consumption — mitigation): src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:
- cosine_similarity(['not-a-number'],['x']) -> TypeError: must be real number, not str
- cosine_similarity([None],[1.0]) -> TypeError: must be real number, not NoneType
- cosine_similarity([float('nan')],[1.0]) -> ValueError: vector components must be finite; got nan (via _check_finite, primitives.py:28-34, audit #B6).

In-engine flow (no numeric consumption): pipeline.py:324-347 builds the matrix, checks dedup (330) and ghost ids (334), records provenance with feature_names/n_units/provides only (341-347), and pipeline.py:575 returns it in StudyResult.feature_matrices (dataclass field at pipeline.py:175). No engine code reads fm.rows numerically (grep: only contracts/features.py count/width checks and primitives.cosine_similarity_matrix signature reference rows).

Consistency precedent for the suggested fix: schema.py:77-89 (Corpus __post_init__ dedup + dangling-relation check) comments it "mirrors the duplicate-id and FeatureMatrix checks"; confirm._validate_finite (confirm.py:87-88) and primitives finite guards establish the finite-float discipline the fix would extend to FeatureMatrix.

content.text: I've completed the independent verification. The finding is real but mis-severitized; I've recorded my structured assessment (is_real=true, severity=LOW with full reasoning and evidence).
